content.text: A self-service eval runner is a platform tool that allows agent service teams to define, run, and review automated evaluation suites for their agent systems without requiring involvement from the platform team for each run. Teams write eval test cases (input-output pairs with associated scoring criteria), register them with the eval runner, and then trigger runs from their CI pipeline, from the command line, or from a web portal. The runner executes each test case against the agent's current implementation, scores the output, compares results to a baseline, and produces a dashboard report and a CI gate signal indicating whether quality has regressed.

Self-service eval tooling differs from general-purpose testing frameworks in two key ways: it is designed for non-deterministic, LLM-generated outputs (not exact matches), and it maintains a history of run results that enables trend analysis rather than just pass/fail at a point in time. The tooling supports multiple scorer types — exact match for structured outputs, LLM-as-judge for subjective quality, task success metrics for agentic workflows — and manages the infrastructure concerns (API key allocation for eval runs, parallel execution, result storage) so that teams focus on the eval content rather than the mechanics.

Agent system quality is difficult to assess without automated eval tooling. Manual testing of a prompt change does not tell you whether the change improved quality on the distribution of real inputs or only on the cases the developer tested. Regression testing without automated evals requires comparing outputs by hand, which does not scale to dozens of test cases per PR across multiple teams. Without evals, teams rely on production monitoring to detect quality regressions — finding problems after they affect users rather than before.

Self-service tooling is specifically important because evals are high-iteration work: a team tuning a prompt may run fifty eval cycles in a day, each time slightly adjusting the prompt and checking whether quality improved or regressed. If each run requires submitting a platform ticket or waiting for a platform-managed CI job to queue, the iteration speed drops by an order of magnitude. Self-service runs that return results in under five minutes enable the tight feedback loop that prompt engineering and agent behavior tuning require.
Teams can iterate on prompt and behavior changes with fast eval feedback rather than waiting for production signals.
CI gate integration prevents quality-regressing changes from merging to main.
Historical run records enable trend analysis: is quality improving, stable, or degrading over time?
LLM-as-judge scoring enables assessment of qualitative properties (helpfulness, tone, completeness) that cannot be captured by exact match.
Baseline pinning makes regression detection precise: a 2% score drop on a specific rubric is detectable, not lost in noise.
Shared infrastructure for parallel execution and result storage reduces the overhead of running large eval suites.
LLM-as-judge scoring introduces a second LLM call per test case, adding cost and latency to eval runs.
Maintaining high-quality eval test cases requires ongoing curation effort; stale cases reduce the signal value of eval scores.
Non-deterministic outputs mean that the same eval run can produce different scores; statistical approaches are needed for rigorous regression detection.
Teams may optimize their agents to score well on the eval suite rather than on real user needs if eval cases are not representative.
Large eval suites (hundreds of cases) can be expensive to run frequently; balancing coverage with cost requires discipline.

Teams define eval suites as structured configuration files (YAML or JSON) that specify: the list of test cases (each with an input and a reference output or a success criterion), the scorer type for each case, the baseline run ID to compare against, and the regression threshold (e.g., fail if score drops more than 3 percentage points from baseline). The eval suite configuration is committed to the team's service repository alongside application code, so eval changes go through the same review process as code changes.

When a run is triggered, the eval runner loads the suite configuration, fetches the agent's current implementation (via API endpoint or by running the agent locally in a sandbox), and executes each test case in parallel using a worker pool. For each case, the runner submits the input to the agent, collects the output, and passes the output to the specified scorer. Scorers can be deterministic (regex match, JSON schema validation, tool call verification) or LLM-based (the runner submits the input, expected output, and actual output to a judge model with a scoring rubric). Scores are aggregated across all cases and compared to the baseline.

Results are stored in the eval results database with a run ID, timestamp, commit SHA, suite version, and per-case scores. The runner produces a summary report with the overall score, the change from baseline, and the list of cases that regressed or improved. In CI integration mode, the runner exits with a non-zero status code if the regression threshold is exceeded, blocking PR merge. In interactive mode, the results are displayed in the portal with detailed per-case breakdowns.

Introduce eval tooling when a team begins making regular changes to prompt templates, model selection, or agent behavior and needs feedback faster than production monitoring can provide. For a new agent service, basic eval cases (smoke tests covering the most common usage patterns) should be created during initial development, before the service is in production, so that the first production deployment has a quality baseline established.

Invest in LLM-as-judge scoring when the agent's output quality is the primary product concern (as in summarization, question answering, or multi-step reasoning services). For services where the primary concern is task completion rate or tool usage accuracy, task success metrics are more informative than output quality scores and less expensive than LLM-judge evaluation.

# evals/suite.yaml — eval suite definition committed to service repo
suite_name: doc-summarizer-v1
description: Evaluation suite for document summarization agent
baseline_run_id: run_20250601_abc123  # pinned baseline for regression detection
regression_threshold: 0.03  # fail CI if score drops more than 3 percentage points

test_cases:
  - id: tc_001
    description: Short article summarization
    input:
      document: "The European Space Agency announced..."  # truncated
      max_sentences: 3
    scorer:
      type: llm_judge
      rubric: "Does the summary capture the main facts? Is it under 3 sentences? Score 0-1."
      judge_model: claude-3-5-haiku-20241022

  - id: tc_002
    description: Technical document key points extraction
    input:
      document: "This RFC describes a new protocol..."
      mode: key_points
    scorer:
      type: llm_judge
      rubric: "Does the output list the key technical decisions? Score 0-1."
      judge_model: claude-3-5-haiku-20241022

  - id: tc_003
    description: Structured output format check
    input:
      document: "Q3 earnings exceeded expectations..."
      output_format: json
    scorer:
      type: json_schema
      schema:
        type: object
        required: [summary, sentiment, key_figures]

# evals/run.py — local eval run script
import subprocess, json, sys, os

EVAL_RUNNER_URL = os.environ.get(
    "EVAL_RUNNER_URL", "https://eval-runner.internal.company.com"
)
SERVICE_ENDPOINT = os.environ.get("SERVICE_ENDPOINT", "http://localhost:8080")

def run_eval(suite_path: str = "evals/suite.yaml") -> dict:
    result = subprocess.run([
        "eval-runner", "run",
        "--suite", suite_path,
        "--target-url", SERVICE_ENDPOINT,
        "--output", "json",
    ], capture_output=True, text=True)

    if result.returncode not in (0, 1):  # 0=pass, 1=regression, others=error
        print(f"Eval runner error: {result.stderr}")
        sys.exit(2)

    report = json.loads(result.stdout)
    print(f"Score: {report['overall_score']:.3f} (baseline: {report['baseline_score']:.3f})")
    print(f"Delta: {report['score_delta']:+.3f}")

    if report.get("regression_detected"):
        print("REGRESSION DETECTED — cases that regressed:")
        for case in report["regressed_cases"]:
            print(f"  {case['id']}: {case['baseline_score']:.2f} → {case['score']:.2f}")
    return report

if __name__ == "__main__":
    report = run_eval()
    sys.exit(1 if report.get("regression_detected") else 0)

# .github/workflows/eval.yml — CI integration
name: Eval Suite
on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'src/**'
      - 'prompts/**'
      - 'evals/**'

jobs:
  eval:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Start service in sandbox
        run: docker compose up -d --wait
        env:
          LLM_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.EVAL_SANDBOX_API_KEY }}

      - name: Run eval suite
        run: python evals/run.py
        env:
          SERVICE_ENDPOINT: http://localhost:8080
          EVAL_RUNNER_URL: ${{ secrets.EVAL_RUNNER_URL }}

      - name: Upload results
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: eval-results
          path: eval-results.json

A team making a change to the summarization prompt for their doc-summarizer service runs their eval suite locally before creating a PR: `python evals/run.py`. The run completes in 90 seconds (12 cases, parallel execution). The report shows an overall score of 0.88, up from the baseline of 0.82 — the prompt change improved quality. The team creates a PR; the CI eval job confirms the improvement and the PR is marked as quality-improving in the review summary.

A second change to add sentence length constraints to the prompt is evaluated locally. The run shows the overall score drops from 0.82 to 0.76 — below the regression threshold. The runner identifies three test cases that regressed: cases where long documents now produce truncated summaries that miss key facts. The developer adjusts the constraint logic to apply only to single-sentence summaries and re-runs the eval. The new score is 0.83, no regression, and the PR is submitted.

The platform team uses the historical run data to produce a monthly quality trends report for all agent services that have eval suites. The report shows that four services have seen consistent score improvements over the quarter (prompt tuning is paying off), two services have stable scores (maintenance mode), and one service has a declining trend (a model upgrade in week 8 caused a regression that was not caught by evals at the time because the eval suite did not cover that failure mode). The team with the declining trend is prompted to add coverage for the uncovered failure mode.

OpenAI's evals framework (open-sourced on GitHub as `openai/evals`) implements this model: teams define eval suites as YAML files with test cases, specify a completion function that runs the model, and use built-in scorers (match, includes, fuzzy match) or write custom scorer functions. The framework handles parallel execution and result aggregation. Several companies using the OpenAI evals framework for internal quality tracking have adapted it into internal self-service tooling by adding CI integration, baseline management, result dashboards, and organizational access controls. Anthropic's own internal eval tooling follows similar principles, enabling Anthropic teams to run thousands of eval cases against Claude models for capability assessment and regression detection.
Writing eval cases that are too similar to each other, so the suite only covers a narrow slice of actual usage and misses common failure modes.
Using LLM-as-judge for every case regardless of whether exact match would be more appropriate and less expensive.
Not pinning a baseline run, so 'regression' is evaluated against the current run each time rather than against a stable historical reference.
Letting eval suites grow large without pruning stale or redundant cases, increasing run cost and time without adding signal value.
Running evals in an environment with production API keys, creating a cost risk if the suite accidentally generates a large number of LLM calls.
Treating eval scores as the definitive quality measure rather than as one signal among several; production user feedback and task success rates provide complementary signals.
Start with ten to twenty carefully selected test cases that cover the most important capability dimensions and common failure modes; expand gradually based on observed production failures.
Use the cheapest suitable scorer for each case: exact match first, task success metric second, LLM judge only when necessary.
Pin the baseline at a stable commit (e.g., the last production release) rather than the previous CI run, so regression is detected relative to production quality, not just the previous PR.
Budget eval run costs explicitly: track cost per run and set an alert if a suite run exceeds a cost threshold, so large eval suites do not accidentally become expensive.
Review eval cases quarterly alongside production failure analysis; add cases for recurring production failure modes, retire cases that have never failed in 12 months.
Make eval results visible to the whole team, not just the author of the change; quality trends are a shared responsibility.

Eval run time is proportional to the number of test cases multiplied by the time per case. For LLM-based scorers, each case requires at least two LLM calls (one for the agent, one for the judge). With a target run time of under five minutes, a suite with 100 cases and 2-second latency per LLM call requires a concurrency of roughly 40 parallel workers. Design the eval runner's worker pool to scale horizontally so that large suites complete within the target time even on CI infrastructure with resource constraints.

Result storage scales linearly with the number of runs times the number of cases per run. For teams running evals on every PR, a service with 100 cases and 10 PRs per day accumulates 1,000 case results per day. After six months, the database holds 180,000 case results — small enough to query efficiently without partitioning. For larger organizations running thousands of PRs per day across many services, result storage partitioning by service and time window becomes necessary.

Eval runs execute agent code against LLM APIs in a sandbox environment. The sandbox must use dedicated eval-tier API keys with lower quotas than production keys, so a runaway eval suite does not exhaust the team's production quota. Network policies in the eval sandbox should restrict outbound connections to only the approved LLM API endpoints and the eval runner service — eval code should not be able to make arbitrary outbound requests during execution.

Test case inputs in the eval suite may contain sensitive reference data (real documents or queries from production logs that have been anonymized). The eval results store must apply the same data classification controls as the original data. Do not store raw eval inputs and outputs in a publicly accessible artifact store; use the internal eval runner's result storage with access controls that match the team's data handling requirements.
Self-service eval tooling enables the fast iteration loops that prompt engineering and agent behavior tuning require.
Eval suites belong in the service repository alongside application code, version-controlled and reviewed like any other change.
Pin a stable baseline and set a regression threshold to make CI gate failures objective rather than subjective.
Choose scorers by cost-effectiveness: exact match before task success before LLM judge, based on what each case actually needs.
Historical run records enable quality trend analysis that production monitoring alone cannot provide.
Run evals with sandbox API keys and restricted network access to prevent cost overruns and unintended external calls.
